item.title: 数字服务法 (DSA) 及其如何改变一切 | The Digital Services Act (DSA) and How it Could Change Everything
item.description:
数字服务法案 (DSA) 是欧盟委员会的一项提案，于 2020 年 12 月提交欧洲议会审议。该提案的一个主要目的是加强对互联网上非法和声名狼藉的内容的监管，使托管网站负责。 我们应该记住，这项仍有待投票的立法是自 2000 年以来对电子商务指令的重大更新。DSA 是本着英国 2019 年在线危害白皮书的精神颁布的，该白皮书规定让互联网公司对托管有问题的内容负责的阶段。

还有一项配套提案，即《数字市场法》（DMA），专门旨在促进与技术垄断企业的竞争。 尽管该立法是欧洲立法，但如果获得批准，它将为全球监管定下基调——这就是互联网互联互通的本质。 与GDPR 一样，拥有国际用户群的美国公司（如 Facebook 和 Google）将受到欧洲立法的影响。

在这一点上，想象一下在像戴德伍德这样的狂野西部小镇建立正义，你就会大致了解我们所处的领域是多么的未知。内容应该如何监管，谁应该监管，什么是应该对违规行为进行处罚，是否应该对其进行监管，都是持续存在的问题。

根据该提案，欧盟将能够处以罚款，而无需通过法院。 值得注意的是，如果发现公司故意无视市场竞争规则，相应的DMA将被处以最高10%的罚款。

下架

那么，网络平台需要自律吗？ 好吧，这对公司来说可能是很多工作，而且可能看起来有点独裁。 因此，DSA 构思了一个相当聪明的解决方案来平息言论自由冲击的光学效应，同时试图将一些控制权交还给用户——这双手已经从科技巨头的冰山上滑落了很长一段时间。 该法案的解决方案是让用户标记可能不适当的内容，有效地将其引起平台的注意，从而使平台对材料作出反应——我们所说的反应是指迅速删除它。 这是立法可以使用更多细微差别的地方。 公司的责任在某些东西被标记的那一刻开始，因此他们或多或少地自动被迫删除内容以保护自己。 这种企业行为似乎与现代取消文化的面子策略相称：先取消，然后再提问，在这种情况下， EDRi和EFF等各种倡导团体已经对这项政策提出异议。

公平地说，DSA 还打算防止不合理的删除索赔，支持删除扩散它们的用户，以免互联网成为一个不分青红皂白的大型告密池。 为此，DSA 支持与“ 受信任的举报者”合作，他们实际上将获得对公司数据的访问权限。 大型科技公司还必须进行自我审计，由独立研究人员进行检查。 虽然这个过程的大部分内容都是以阻止非法或有害内容的传播为幌子，但它真正要做的是让政府乃至整个世界更深入地了解大型科技公司庞大且通常难以理解的复杂数据实践。 然而，值得注意的是，当这些公司收集的数据落入更多人手中时，尽管他们可能是受信任的，但泄漏/泄露的可能性就会增加。

上述倡导团体支持的提案方面之一是希望提高透明度，不仅包括内容删除推理，还包括有针对性的广告和内容推荐。 如果要求，平台将被罚款以公开发布其算法，这让公司感到沮丧，因为这有点像要求快餐连锁店透露其秘方。

大扫除技术的大扫除立法？

在过去的 20 年中，互联网连接世界的方式使全球立法问题比以往任何事情都复杂得多。 因此，欧盟希望其“布鲁塞尔效应”，即通过欧盟传播到欧洲的法治，将全面解决这个问题。

但没那么快。

不要忘记（你怎么可能）英国已经退出了欧罗巴俱乐部，并且不会像剩下的欧盟那样受制于 DSA。

然后让我们看一下，比如说，德国，一个在各个领域都有自己的法规的主要欧盟国家。 虽然 DSA/DMA 仍在谈判中，但德国已经通过了自己的数字竞争修正案(GMB)。 随着巴黎、罗马、柏林和华沙急切地敦促Vestiger 的立法生效，德国正在努力控制科技巨头并增加竞争。

毕竟，对整个欧盟进行监管确实有一个有趣的经济目的。 根据维斯塔格专员的说法，欧洲没有像美国或中国那样发展自己的科技巨头的原因是，它的市场被不同的欧洲公司分割，每个公司都有各自的法规。 像 DSA 和 DMA 这样的立法将有助于将欧洲变成像美国或中国这样的单一技术市场，这是一个广泛的基础，可以从中发展自己的竞争技术。

与此同时，如果 DSA 获得通过，世界科技巨头将不得不适应一种新的事态，我们只能希望这种新状态会接近不可磨灭的作品《奥斯汀鲍尔斯：国际神秘人》中描述的乌托邦。 “自由与责任”的世界——“一个非常美妙的时代”。



原文:

The Digital Services Act (DSA) is a proposal by the European Commission, submitted for consideration to the European parliament in December 2020. One main aim of the proposal is to increase the regulation of illegal and disreputable content on the internet, making the hosting sites accountable. The legislation, which we should remember is still to be voted on, is a significant update to the e-Commerce Directive from way back in the year 2000. The DSA is an enactment in the spirit of Britain’s 2019 Online Harms White Paper, which set the stage for making internet companies liable for hosting problematic content.

The DSA legislation is lead by Margrethe Vestager and Thierry Breton, EU commissioners known for their stance against big tech. There is also a companion proposal, the Digital Markets Act (DMA), specifically aimed at promoting competition against tech monopolies. Though the legislation is European, if ratified, it will set the tone for worldwide regulation — such is the nature of the internet’s interconnectivity. As with the GDPR, companies based in America with international user bases, like Facebook and Google, will be affected by European legislation.

The impact of the DSA

The DSA is the next big step in the ongoing internet regulation conversation, a prominent facet of which is the right to free speech. At this point, envision something like the establishment of justice in a Wild West town like Deadwood, and you’ll have a general idea of how uncharted a territory we’re in. How content should be regulated, who should regulate it, what the penalties should be for infractions, and whether it should even be regulated, are all ongoing questions.

According to the proposal, the EU would be able to impose fines without having to go through the courts. The DSA stipulates that the largest companies (those with 45+ million users), can be fined up to six percent of their annual revenue if they are found hosting hate speech or the sale of illegal goods. It’s worth noting that the corresponding DMA will fine up to ten percent if a company is found to have deliberately disregarded market competition rules.

The takedown

So, do online platforms need to police themselves? Well that might be a lot of work for the companies and also might seem a bit dictatorial. The DSA has thus conceived of a rather clever solution to quell the optics of free-speech impingement while attempting to put some control back in users’ hands — the same hands which have been slipping off the big tech iceberg for quite some time now. The act’s solution is to have users flag potentially inappropriate content, effectively bringing it to the platform’s attention, thus causing the platform to react to the material — and by react we mean quickly removing it. This is where the legislation could use more nuance. The companies’ liability kicks in the moment something is flagged, so they are more or less automatically compelled to remove the content to protect themselves. It’s corporate action that seems commensurate with the modern day face-saving tactics of cancel culture: cancel first, ask questions later, and in this instance it’s a policy that various advocacy groups like the EDRi and the EFF have already taken issue with.

To be fair, the DSA also intends to protect against unjustified takedown claims, supporting the removal of users who proliferate them, lest the internet become one big indiscriminate snitch-pool. To that end, the DSA supports working with “trusted flaggers”, who will actually gain access to the company’s data. The large tech companies will also have to perform a self-audit, which will be checked by independent researchers. While much of this process is under the guise of stopping the spread of illegal or harmful content, what it will really do is give the government and perhaps the world at large more insight into the vast and often impenetrably complex data practices of large tech companies. However, it’s worth noting that when the data collected by these companies gets into more hands, trusted though they may be, there is an increase in the leak/breach possibilities.

One of the proposal’s aspects which the aforementioned advocacy groups do favor is the desire for increased transparency around not only the content takedown reasoning, but targeted advertising and content recommendation. If requested, platforms are required by penalty of fine to publish their algorithms publicly, which is a source of dismay for the companies, since it’s kind of like asking a fast food chain to reveal its secret sauce recipe.

Big sweeping legislation for big sweeping tech?

In the last twenty years, the internet has connected the world in ways that have created global legislation issues exponentially more complex than anything seen before. The EU is thus hoping that its “Brussels Effect”, the rule of law spread over Europe via the EU, will take care of the problem in blanket fashion.

But not so fast.

Don’t forget (how could you) the U.K. has exited Club Europa, and won’t be as beholden to the DSA as the remaining EU.

And then let’s take a look at, say, Germany, a major EU country that is rife with its own regulation in various sectors. While the DSA/DMA are lingering in negotiation, Germany has passed its own digital competition amendment (GMB). As Paris, Rome, Berlin, and Warsaw eagerly urge Vestiger’s legislation into existence, Germany is taking a stab at reining in tech giants and increasing competition.

Throwing a regulatory blanket over the entire EU does after all have an interesting economic purpose. According to Commissioner Vestager, the reason that Europe hasn’t grown its own tech giants on par with those in America or China is that it’s had a market fragmented by different European companies each with their individual regulations. Legislation like the DSA and DMA would help turn Europe into a single tech market like the US or China, a widespread base from which to grow its own competing tech.

In the meantime, though, if the DSA is passed, the world’s tech behemoths will have to adjust to a new state of affairs that we can only hope will approximate the utopia described in the indelible work, Austin Powers: International Man of Mystery, a world of “Freedom and responsibility”– “a very groovy time.”
item.author: bunko_security

item.title: 下一个 13：有什么新功能？ | Next 13: What's new?
item.description:
纪尧姆·杜汉

发表于大家好，

我对这个新版本感到非常兴奋。 所以我制作了这个简短的视频来讨论什么是新的。

快速简历：

  • 一个新的路由系统，
  • 我们摆脱了 getServerSideProps / getStaticProps ！

但是我们仍然不知道 API ......还是我错过了什么？

观看我的视频，快速了解 Next 13！

最好的，

纪尧姆

原文:

Guillaume Duhan

Posted on Oct 30

Hello everyone,

Vercel's conference happened this week and we discovered Next 13!

I was so excited about this new version. So I made this quick video to talk about what was new.

A quick resume:

  • a new routing system,
  • layout.js, page.js, loading.js, error.js...,
  • turbopack will rocks — and may replace webpack,
  • we get rid of getServerSideProps / getStaticProps !

But we still don't know for APIs... Or did I miss something?

Watch my video for a quick presentation of Next 13!

Best,

Guillaume

Anurag Vishwakarma - Oct 10

item.title: Hacktoberfest 总结：对 Linked 的贡献不大 | Hacktoberfest Wrap-Up: Minor Contribution to Linked
item.description:
三天，1个PR？ 这很令人痛心。

幸运的是，我有机会为Linked项目做出贡献。

关于联动

问题

因此，此时在包含拼写错误的单词下划线是没有意义的。 您可以在此处阅读有关该问题的更多信息。

修复

解决方案是通过将其作为值为false.的prop传递来禁用拼写检查。 请参阅此处的 PR。

遇到的挑战

  • 熟悉 Vue
  • 在node版本 18.2 上运行 Linked 时出现问题。 我必须降级到版本 16 才能在我的机器上运行

结果

如果您拼错单词，编辑不再抱怨。

最后的想法

  • 总的来说，我很高兴为不同的项目做出贡献，并与全球的开发人员一起工作
  • 我了解到，如果我真的有兴趣在日常生活中使用它，那么为项目做出贡献会更容易。
  • 一个小小的贡献就足以显着增强我的信心。
  • 这样做是为了体验，而不是福利。

一旦暂停，tdaw 将无法评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被解除。

一旦解除暂停，tdaw 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，tdaw 的所有帖子都将被隐藏，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 tdaw 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

感谢您保护 DEV 社区👩‍💻👨‍💻 的安全。 以下是标记 tdaw 的方法：

举报其他不当行为

取消标记 tdaw 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

Three days, 1 PR? That's distressing.

Luckily, I got an opportunity to contribute to the Linked project.

About Linked

Linked enables users to declutter their minds and record their thoughts through journaling.

The Issue

Linked is a reasonably new journaling tool that has yet to implement the spell-check feature. Therefore, there is no point in underlining words containing typos at this point. You can read more about the issue here.

The Fix

The solution was to disable spell-check by passing it as prop with the value of false. See the PR here.

Challenges Encountered

  • Familiarizing myself with Vue
  • Trouble running Linked on node version 18.2. I had to downgrade to version 16 to make it work on my machine

Outcome

The editor no longer complains if you misspell words.

Final Thoughts

  • Overall, I had fun contributing to different projects and working with developers around the globe
  • I learned that it is easier to contribute to a project if I am genuinely interested in using it in my everyday life.
  • A minor contribution is enough to boost my confidence significantly.
  • Do it for the experience, not the perks.

Once unsuspended, tdaw will be able to comment and publish posts again.

Once unpublished, all posts by tdaw will become hidden and only accessible to themselves.

item.title: 叫醒电话：为什么急需处理您的硬编码凭据 | Wake-up call: why it's urgent to deal with your hardcoded credentials
item.description:
今天很明显，2021 年将载入 IT 安全史册，因为组织真正意识到他们不可避免地依赖开源，更重要的是，意识到无监督供应链带来的风险。

如今，企业、开源项目、非营利基金会，甚至政府都在试图弄清楚如何提高全球软件供应链的安全性。 虽然这些努力非常受欢迎，但目前，组织几乎没有任何直接的方法可以在这方面进行改进。

另一方面，尽管在很大程度上被认为是黑客最常见的切入点之一，但仍有一种漏洞在很大程度上没有受到关注：源代码中的硬编码凭据。

在本文中，我们要捍卫一个简单的事实：专注于您现在可以控制的内容可以极大地改善您组织的安全状况。

代码中的秘密是您可以并且应该立即开始监控的东西。 这就是为什么。

硬编码的秘密从未如此简单

错误？ 据称，一个包含个人信息数据库秘密的源代码片段被复制并粘贴到中国 CSDN 网络的开发者博客上。 资源

复制到 CSDN 博客的代码片段包含可能导致大规模泄露的关键机密。

无论是由于恶意或疏忽而将秘密留在代码中，它们始终是黑客的福音。 在发现泄露的凭证时，可以部署各种剧本，即使是最无害的凭证，例如 Twitter API 密钥：从鱼叉式网络钓鱼，到权限升级和数据泄露。

秘密（用户名和密码、API 令牌、加密密钥等）是网络犯罪分子最抢手的数字资产，而且它们从未如此容易找到：去年，我们检测到有 600 万个秘密被推送（大部分是无意的），因为在公共 GitHub 上提交，是 2020 年检测到的数量的两倍。根据最新的DBIR 报告，“使用被盗凭据”是迄今为止破坏 Web 应用程序的最常见方式，超过 80% 的违规行为归因于这种攻击向量，而“漏洞利用”直接负责不到 20% 的案例。

使用被盗凭证的策略在 BWAA（基本 Web 应用程序攻击）中尤为持久，DBIR 团队将其定义为“直接”针对暴露实例（例如 Web 服务器和电子邮件服务器）的参与者。 他们还将其称为“低成本、高回报的策略”，这对一系列攻击者具有吸引力。

资源

当然，这里的“被盗凭据”一词涵盖了多种情况，可能包括网络钓鱼和在暗网上购买的用户信息。 但是，如果我们考虑一个制作数字服务和产品的组织，这个结论适用于代码中的秘密。 事实上，对于一个代码生成组织来说，确保源代码中的秘密应该与实施 SSO 和 MFA 一样明显。

在同一份报告中，我们可以看到：

自 2017 年以来，被盗凭据增加了近 30%，巩固了它作为过去四年来获得组织访问权限的最可靠方法之一。

使用被盗或泄露的凭据仍然是数据泄露的最常见原因。 在 2022 年的研究中，被盗或泄露的凭证是 19% 的违规行为的主要攻击媒介，也是 2021 年研究中的首要攻击媒介，造成了 20% 的违规行为。 由被盗或泄露的凭证造成的违规平均损失为 450 万美元。 这些漏洞的生命周期最长——识别漏洞需要 243 天，控制漏洞需要 84 天。 网络钓鱼是导致违规的第二大常见原因，占 16%，也是成本最高的，平均违规成本为 491 万美元。

那么如何解释代码中的秘密仍然是应用程序安全领域中最容易被忽视的漏洞之一呢？ 我们得出的结论是，与其他应用程序安全漏洞相比，硬编码的秘密仍然知之甚少。

秘密不是运行时漏洞（它们更糟）

查看 2022 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Weaknesses列表，我们可以看到“硬编码凭证的使用”（ CWE-798 ）从前一年的第 16 位上升到第 15 位。 但这里最有趣的事实与其说是排名：不如说是，与列表中的所有其他“弱点”不同，使用硬编码机密不是执行漏洞。 换句话说，它不需要运行的软件就是漏洞。

当我们听到应用程序漏洞时，我们习惯于想到跨站请求伪造（CSRF）、服务器端请求伪造（SSRF）、XML外部实体（XXE）、逻辑缺陷等。它们都要求软件是为了被利用而奔跑。 使用硬编码的凭据，可以利用的是源代码本身。 因此，您的攻击面包括您的存储库和整个软件工厂。 这是一个具有重大意义的真正独特的特征。

首先，硬编码的凭据去源代码去的地方，使得跟踪几乎不可能。 源代码通常每月在组织内部或外部的机器上进行多次克隆、签出和分叉——更不用说泄漏事件了。

确实会发生泄漏。 去年，在他们的代码库公开曝光后，我们使用我们用来保护客户的相同工具检查了Twitch和三星的回购协议。 在这两种情况下，我们都发现了 6,500 到 7,000 个可供使用的秘密：从公司电子邮件密码到云服务 API 密钥、第三方令牌或内部服务身份验证。 如果这也发生在NVIDIA和Microsoft身上，您认为不会发生在您的组织中吗？

其次，我们不要忘记VCS 控制下的代码具有永久的历史。 诸如 git 之类的 VCS 将跟踪对代码库所做的任何修改，并且还用于传播这些更改。 再加上硬编码凭证只要不被撤销就​​可以被利用，这意味着仍然有效的秘密可以隐藏在 VCS 历史时间线上的任何地方。 这为攻击面打开了一个新的维度，大多数安全分析永远不会看到，因为它们只关心代码库的当前、准备部署的状态。

因此，与任何其他类型的漏洞不同，硬编码凭证会随着时间累积。 就像技术债务一样，管理这个漏洞是一场俄罗斯方块游戏： 而且开发人员越多（过去、现在或将来），秘密已经或将要提交的可能性就越高。 代码库越大，潜在可利用秘密的数量就越多。 过了某个点，它就变得无法管理。

根据我们自己的报告，这是典型软件商店将面临的平均情况：

平均而言，在 2021 年，一家拥有 400 名开发人员和 4 名 AppSec 工程师的典型公司会在扫描其存储库和提交时发现1,050 个泄露的独特机密。 每个秘密平均在 13 个不同的地方检测到，修复所需的工作量远远超过当前 AppSec 团队的能力（1 个 AppSec 工程师为 100 名开发人员提供服务）。

这就是为什么开始对秘密蔓延采取行动的正确时机永远是现在（如果不是昨天！）。

如何避免硬编码凭据？

你可以采取什么行动？ 首先实施正确的控制以阻止凭据进入代​​码库（我们经常使用“止血”的比喻）。 实时捕获硬编码的秘密是遏制秘密蔓延进程的最佳方式。 在它们成为提交之前抓住它们。

让我们举个具体的例子：开发人员在他的本地工作环境中错误地提交了一个秘密。 如果他在那一刻收到警报，那么修复的单一成本不到一分钟。 凭证仅在本地工作站上公开，开发人员可以运行一些命令来“修改”他们的提交。 相反，如果凭证到达中央存储库，则应将其视为已泄露，因为任何具有读取访问权限的人、未经授权的开发人员和潜在的 APT 都可以使用它。 从那时起，应该触发一个完整的修复周期。

撤销和轮换密钥通常涉及多个团队。 开发人员可能需要向 Cloud Ops 团队提出请求，而后者又不得不停止该过程中的一些工作流，例如 CI/CD 管道甚至生产工人。

时间加起来很快，根据我们的经验，修复的平均成本通常至少在 2 个工时左右。

数学很简单：对于一个有 400 名开发人员的商店，我们发现了 1,050 个独特的秘密，我们可以估计至少需要 2,100 个工时才能达到“代码中的零秘密”——假设没有更多的秘密会在未来！

底线是，从小处开始但快速增长，而不是从一开始就尝试解决所有问题（尤其是过去事件的“存量”），您的投资回报率会好得多。

专注于解决过去的事件而不采取任何措施来防止新的事件不会产生降低暴露秘密总数的预期结果，进而减少未来补救所需的工作量。

根据我们的经验，设置正确的护栏可以为 AppSec 团队释放大量资源，而不会造成不必要的摩擦，因为它不再需要每月处理数百个经过验证的事件。 你可以在这篇文章中阅读更多关于它的信息：

不要睡在秘密上

实时跟上网络安全威胁不仅困难，而且是不可能的。 不仅每天都会出现新的对手和 TTP，而且近年来成功攻击的门槛已经大大降低，以至于看到青少年接管一些最大的科技公司也就不足为奇了。

我们认为信息安全是一个复杂的领域，不应根据意见、直觉或简单的恐惧做出决定。 但我们认为，尽管基础知识一直被认为是大多数违规行为的根本原因，但我们经常会忽视这些基础知识。 对于开发人员编写代码的组织，必须对机密进行严格监督，以免向恶意人员提供唾手可得的果实。

处理硬编码凭证是当务之急，因为组织等待的时间越长，情况就越危险，安全债务的成本也越高。

独角兽开发者 - 一旦暂停，gitguardian 将无法评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被解除。

一旦解除暂停，gitguardian 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦未发布，gitguardian 的所有帖子都将被隐藏，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 gitguardian 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

感谢您保护 DEV 社区👩‍💻👨‍💻 的安全。 以下是标记 gitguardian 的方法：

举报其他不当行为

取消标记 gitguardian 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

It is clear today that the year 2021 will go down in the annals of IT security as the year when organizations really became aware of their inevitable dependence on open-source, and more importantly, of the risks posed by unsupervised supply chains.

High-profile security incidents like the SolarWinds, Kaseya, and Codecov data breaches have shaken enterprises’ confidence in the security practices of third-party service providers.

Today corporations, open source projects, nonprofit foundations, and even governments are all trying to figure out how to improve the global software supply chain security. While these efforts are more than welcome, for the moment, there is hardly any straightforward way for organizations to improve on that front.

On the other hand, despite being largely acknowledged as one of the most common entry points for hackers, a type of vulnerability remains largely unwatched: hard-coded credentials in source code.

In this article, we want to defend a simple fact: focusing on what you can control now can greatly improve your organization's security posture.

Secrets-in-the code is something you can and should start monitoring now. Here's why.

Hard-coded secrets have never been easier to find

On July 3, 2022, the CEO of crypto-currency giant Binance warned of a massive breach:

The bug? A fragment of source code containing the secret for a titanic database of personal information was allegedly copied and pasted onto a developer's blog of the Chinese CSDN network. Source

Code snippet copied to the CSDN blog contained a critical secret possibly causing a massive breach.

No matter if secrets have been left in code because of malintent or negligence, they are always a boon for hackers. All sorts of playbooks can be deployed upon finding leaked credentials, even the most innocuous ones, such as a Twitter API key: from spearphishing, to privilege escalation and data exfiltration.

Secrets (username and passwords, API tokens, encryption keys, etc.) are the most sought-after digital assets for cybercriminals and they have never been easier to find: last year, we detected that 6 million secrets were pushed (mostly inadvertently) as commits on public GitHub, twice the amount detected in 2020. According to the latest DBIR report, the “use of stolen credentials” is by far the most common way to breach web applications, with more than 80% of the breaches attributed to this attack vector, while “vulnerability exploitation” is directly responsible for less than 20% of the cases.

The tactic of using stolen credentials was particularly persistent in BWAA (Basic Web Application Attacks), which the DBIR team defined as an actor "directly" targeting exposed instances, such as web servers and email servers. They also referred to it as a "low-cost, high-pay-off strategy," which is attractive to an array of attackers.

Source

Of course, here the term "stolen credentials" encompasses a variety of cases, including probably phishing and user info bought on the dark web. But if we consider an organization crafting digital services and products, this conclusion applies to secrets-in-code. In fact, for a code-producing organization, making sure secrets are kept out of source code should be as evident as implementing SSO and MFA.

In the same report, we can read that:

There’s been an almost 30% increase in stolen credentials since 2017, cementing it as one of the most tried-and-true methods to gain access to an organization for the past four years.

Another report from IBM came to the same conclusion:

Use of stolen or compromised credentials remains the most common cause of a data breach. Stolen or compromised credentials were the primary attack vector in 19% of breaches in the 2022 study and also the top attack vector in the 2021 study, having caused 20% of breaches. Breaches caused by stolen or compromised credentials had an average cost of USD 4.50 million. These breaches had the longest lifecycle — 243 days to identify the breach and another 84 days to contain the breach. Phishing was the second most common cause of a breach at 16% and also the costliest, averaging USD 4.91 million in breach costs.

How to explain then that secrets-in-code is still one of the most overlooked vulnerabilities in the application security space? We have come to the conclusion that hard-coded secrets are still poorly understood compared to other application security vulnerabilities.

Secrets are not a runtime vulnerability (they’re much worse)

Looking at the 2022 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Weaknesses list, we can see that "Use of Hard-coded Credentials" (CWE-798) is in position 15, up from 16 in the previous year. But the most interesting fact here is not so much the ranking: it is rather that, unlike all the other "weaknesses" on the list, the use of hard-coded secrets is not an execution vulnerability. In other words, it doesn't require running software to be a vulnerability.

When we hear about application vulnerabilities, we are used to thinking about Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF), Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF), XML External Entity (XXE), logic flaws, etc. They all require the software to be running in order to be exploited. With hard-coded credentials, it's the source code itself that can be exploited. Therefore, your attack surface comprises your repositories and your entire software factory. This is a truly unique characteristic that has big implications.

First, hard-coded credentials go where source code goes, making tracking almost impossible. Source code is usually cloned, checked out, and forked multiple times a month on machines inside or outside an organization's perimeter—not to mention leakage incidents.

Leaks do happen. Last year, after their codebases were exposed publicly, we examined Twitch and Samsung’s repos with the same tool we use to protect our clients. In both cases, we found between 6,500 to 7,000 secrets ready to be employed: from company email passwords, to cloud services API keys, third-party tokens, or internal services authentication. If this also happened to NVIDIA and Microsoft, do you think it can’t happen to your organization?

Second, let's not forget that code under VCS control has a permanent history. A VCS such as git will keep track of any modifications done to a codebase and is also used to propagate these changes. Coupled with the fact that hardcoded credentials will be exploitable as long as they are not revoked, it means that still-valid secrets can be hiding anywhere on the VCS historical timeline. This opens a new dimension to the attack surface, that most security analyses will never see because they are only concerned with the current, ready-to-be-deployed, state of a codebase.

Therefore, unlike any other kind of vulnerability, hardcoded credentials accumulate in time. Much like technical debt, managing this vulnerability is a Tetris game: “It’s ok if it builds up a bit, as long as you have a plan to reduce it later.” Except that you don’t see the bricks until you put detection in place. And the more developers (in the past, present, or future), the higher the probability that a secret has been or will be committed. The bigger the codebase, the higher the number of potentially exploitable secrets.  Past a certain point, it simply becomes unmanageable.

From our own report, this is the average situation a typical software shop will be facing:

On average, in 2021, a typical company with 400 developers and 4 AppSec engineers would discover 1,050 unique secrets leaked upon scanning its repositories and commits. With each secret detected in 13 different places on average, the amount of work required for remediation far exceeds current AppSec teams' capabilities (1 AppSec engineer for 100 developers).

These are the reasons why the right moment to start taking action against secrets sprawl will always be now (if not yesterday!).

How to avoid hard-coded credentials?

What actions can you take? Implement the right controls to stop credentials from entering into the codebase in the first place (we often use the analogy “stop the bleeding”). Catching hardcoded secrets in real-time is the best way to curb the progress of secrets sprawl. Catch them before they even become commits.

Let’s take a concrete example: a developer commits by mistake a secret in his local working environment. If he’s alerted at that moment, the unitary cost of remediation is less than a minute. The credential has only been exposed on the local workstation and the developer can run a few commands to "amend" their commit. Conversely, if the credential reaches the central repository, it should be considered compromised since it became available to anyone with read access, unauthorized developers and potential APTs included. From that point, a full remediation cycle should be triggered.

Revoking and rotating keys typically involve multiple teams. A developer will probably need to address a request to a Cloud Ops team, who will in turn have to halt a few workflows in the process, such as CI/CD pipelines or even production workers.

Time adds up quickly and in our experience average cost of remediation is often at least in the 2 man-hours ballpark.

The maths are straightforward: for a 400 developers shop where we uncovered 1,050 unique secrets, we can approximate that at least 2,100 man-hours would be required to reach “zero secrets-in-code”—assuming that no more secrets would leak in the future!

The bottom line is that your ROI will be much better by starting small but quickly and growing than by trying to fix everything from the start (in particular the “stock” of past incidents).

Focusing on resolving past incidents while doing nothing to prevent new ones will not have the intended results of lowering the total count of exposed secrets and, in turn, reducing the effort needed for remediation in the future.

In our experience, setting up the right guardrails frees considerable resources for the AppSec team without creating unnecessary friction, since it no longer has to deal with hundreds of verified incidents every month. You can read more about it in this article:

How to remediate thousands of hardcoded secrets incidents

Don’t sleep on secrets

Keeping up with cybersecurity threats in real-time is not just difficult, it is impossible. Not only do new adversaries and TTPs emerge daily, but in recent years the bar to a successful attack has been lowered so much that it is not a surprise anymore to see teenagers take over some of the biggest tech companies.

We reckon information security is a complex field and no decision should be made based on opinions, intuition, or simply fear. But we think that too often the basics can be overlooked although they are consistently pointed at for being the root cause of most breaches. For organizations where developers write code, secrets must be well supervised so as not to offer low-hanging fruits to malicious people.

There is an urgency to deal with hard-coded credentials because the more an organization waits, the riskier the situation gets, and the costlier the security debt.

Unicorn Developer - Sep 15

EMQ Technologies - Sep 16

Once suspended, gitguardian will not be able to comment or publish posts until their suspension is removed.

Once unsuspended, gitguardian will be able to comment and publish posts again.

Once unpublished, all posts by gitguardian will become hidden and only accessible to themselves.

If gitguardian is not suspended, they can still re-publish their posts from their dashboard.

Thanks for keeping DEV Community 👩‍💻👨‍💻 safe. Here is what you can do to flag gitguardian:

gitguardian consistently posts content that violates DEV Community 👩‍💻👨‍💻's code of conduct because it is harassing, offensive or spammy.

Report other inappropriate conduct

Unflagging gitguardian will restore default visibility to their posts.
item.author: segudev

item.title: 自动化 AWS Identity Center 权限管理 | Automating AWS Identity Center Permissions Management
item.description:
该帖子介绍了一种解决方案，用于将 AWS Identity Center 组自动化到 AWS 账户，并为在 AWS Identity Center 中从外部身份提供商创建的组分配权限集。

本文假设您熟悉 AWS IAM 服务以及角色和策略等概念。

在 Identity Center 中，权限通过权限集进行管理，而权限集又是 IAM 策略的集合。 将用户或组的权限集分配给账户后，Identity Center 将自动在账户中创建 IAM 角色。 角色的策略是从权限集配置的。 此外，该角色还配置了一个信任策略，该策略允许仅在用户通过联合身份提供者的身份验证后才担任该角色。

对于环境用户的任何任务，管理 AWS 访问控制和权限应始终遵循最低权限概念。 根据对粒度的要求，这可能会导致 IdP 的组与 AWS 账户和权限集的组合之间存在 1:1 的关系。

让我们看一个实际的例子。 对于日常常规操作，大多数任务应该是只读的，属于可见性、监控和观察的范畴。 可以使用 AWS 提供的 AWS 托管策略ReadOnlyAccess ，并且策略定义的权限可以完成工作。

对于账户内环境和 IAM 服务管理的紧急更改，可以使用 AWS 托管AdministratorAccess策略中的策略。

对于需要对环境权限进行交互式更改的任何其他任务，可以使用来自 AWS 托管PowerUserAccess策略的策略。

在示例场景中，IdP 必须拥有三个组，使组的成员可以使用三个权限集ReadOnlyAccess 、 AdministratorAccess和PowerUserAccess访问 AWS 账户 app1-prod。

必须先在 IdP 中创建组并将权限集分配给组的 AWS 账户，然后组中的用户才能登录 AWS 账户。

由于需要经常适应新的应用程序和工作负载，许多组织已经自动创建 AWS 账户。 跨多个 AWS 账户部署工作负载的情况并不少见，每个账户都需要在移交给账户用户之前设置 IAM 身份中心。

解决方案

本文介绍了一种使用 AWS 无服务器资源的解决方案，用于 AWS Identity Center 联合组帐户和权限集分配自动化。 此解决方案假定组的命名遵循常规模式，其中包含目标 AWS 账户和权限集作为组名称的一部分。

作为示例，我们将使用组名aws_accountname_a 。 解决方案实现是基于组名称编码的以下假设构建的：

  • 用于 AWS 账户访问的组的常量前缀（字符串aws_ ）
  • 应分配组的帐户名称（字符串accountname - 整个组名称的_字符之间的任何非空白字符）
  • 应该在账户上分配给组的权限集的短名称（ AWSPowerUserAccess权限集引用的字符串_p ）

先决条件

  • 用户有一个启用了 IAM 身份中心的 AWS 账户。
  • 用户将 IAM 身份中心配置为使用外部身份提供商进行用户和组联合。
  • 外部身份提供者已配置并设置为自动配置，请参阅本文的参考部分。
  • 用户有足够的权限来管理账户内的资源（使用 CloudFormation 或使用其他方法）。

高级架构

下图显示了解决方案的高级架构。

解决方案的工作流程

该解决方案的基本工作流程在以下步骤中突出显示：

  • 外部身份提供商使用身份提供商和 AWS IAM 身份中心之间的 SCIM 协议在 AWS IAM 身份中心内预置一个组，从而触发 AWS CloudTrail 事件。
  • 该函数从事件详细信息中解码组名称，因为它包含所需的帐户名称、要分配给组的所需权限集名称，并在 IAM 身份中心 API 中执行分配。
  • 该过程记录在 AWS CloudWatch 日志组中，用于审计和调试目的。
  • 如果失败，将发送一封电子邮件。

执行

该解决方案在 AWS CloudFormation 中实现，但应该相当可移植，以便在其他基础设施即代码框架（如 Terraform 等）中实现。

主要组件是用于侦听所需事件的 EventBridge 规则、Lambda 函数及其 IAM 权限以及用于在失败时发送电子邮件的 SNS 组件。

该解决方案作为单个 CloudFormation 模板提供，应该很容易部署到启用了 Identity Center 的 AWS 账户。

 Description: AWS SSO Automation Components Parameters: ManagedResourcePrefix: Type: String InstanceArn: Type: String SMTPNotifyAddress: Type: String TopicName: Type: String Default: "AssigmentTopic" Resources: NewSSOGroupEventRule: Type: AWS::Events::Rule Properties: Description: Trigger for when a new SSO Group is propagated from external IdP via SCIM EventPattern: source: - aws.sso-directory detail-type: - AWS API Call via CloudTrail detail: eventSource: - sso-directory.amazonaws.com eventName: - CreateGroup Targets: - Arn: !GetAtt SsoAssignGroupsFunction.Arn Id: sso-assign-group-function ExecutionRole: Type: AWS::IAM::Role Properties: AssumeRolePolicyDocument: Version: 2012-10-17 Statement: - Effect: Allow Principal: Service: lambda.amazonaws.com Action: - sts:AssumeRole ManagedPolicyArns: - arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole Policies: - PolicyName: SSOandOrgPermissions PolicyDocument: Version: 2012-10-17 Statement: - Effect: Allow Action: - sso-directory:Describe* - sso-directory:Get* - sso-directory:List* - sso-directory:Describe* - sso-directory:Search* - sso:Describe* - sso:Get* - sso:List* - sso:CreateAccountAssignment - sso:ProvisionPermissionSet - identitystore:List* - identitystore:Describe* - organizations:ListAccounts - sns:Publish Resource: '*' AssignmentTopic: Type: AWS::SNS::Topic Properties: Subscription: - Endpoint: !Ref SMTPNotifyAddress Protocol: "email" TopicName: !Ref TopicName SsoAssignGroupsFunction: Type: AWS::Lambda::Function Properties: Code: ZipFile: | import re import os import logging import json from time import sleep import boto3 import traceback logger = logging.getLogger() logger.setLevel(logging.DEBUG) # This function assumes that groups are named like aws_<account-name>_<a|r|p> # see the documentation of this function for details. CONST_PREFIX = "aws" g_account_pattern = rf"^{CONST_PREFIX}_(\S*)_(\S*)" PSET_NAME_MAPPING_DICT = { "r": "AWSReadOnlyAccess", "a": "AWSAdministratorAccess", "p": "AWSPowerUserAccess", } sso_admin_client = boto3.client("sso-admin") org_client = boto3.client("organizations") sns_resource = boto3.resource("sns") def list_permission_sets(sso_instance_arn) -> dict: """Returns a dictionary of permissionssets for a given SSO Instance.""" perm_set_dict = {} response = sso_admin_client.list_permission_sets(InstanceArn=sso_instance_arn) results = response["PermissionSets"] while "NextToken" in response: response = sso_admin_client.list_permission_sets( InstanceArn=sso_instance_arn, NextToken=response["NextToken"] ) results.extend(response["PermissionSets"]) for permission_set in results: perm_description = sso_admin_client.describe_permission_set( InstanceArn=sso_instance_arn, PermissionSetArn=permission_set ) perm_set_dict[perm_description["PermissionSet"]["Name"]] = permission_set return perm_set_dict def list_aws_accounts() -> list: """Returns a list of account dictionaries containing name id of each account""" account_list = [] paginator = org_client.get_paginator("list_accounts") page_iterator = paginator.paginate() for page in page_iterator: for acct in page["Accounts"]: # only add active accounts if acct["Status"] == "ACTIVE": data = {"name": acct["Name"], "id": acct["Id"]} account_list.append(data) #logger.debug("List of accounts: %s", account_list) return account_list def lambda_handler(event, context): logger.debug("Invoked with event: %s", event) try: group_display_name = event["detail"]["responseElements"]["group"]["displayName"] if group_display_name == "": logger.debug( "Recieved SCIM CreateGroup event for roup name '%s'", group_display_name ) raise Exception("Event did not contain the group display name property") result = re.search(g_account_pattern, group_display_name) print(result) if not result: logger.error( "Security group: '%s' does not matching naming convention for account assignment. REGEX retourned matches: %s", group_display_name, result, ) raise Exception( "Security group does not match convention for account assignment automation" ) account_name = result.group(1) short_name_pset = result.group(2) # "a" "p" or "r" if short_name_pset not in PSET_NAME_MAPPING_DICT: logger.error( "Short name '%s' for permission set is not known", short_name_pset ) raise Exception("Short name for permission set is not known") permission_set_name = PSET_NAME_MAPPING_DICT[short_name_pset] logger.debug( "Searching for account '%s' and permission set '%s'", account_name, permission_set_name, ) accounts = list_aws_accounts() instance_arn = os.getenv("INSTANCE_ARN") logger.info("IAM Identity Center Instance ARN is configured '%s'", instance_arn) if instance_arn is None: raise Exception("No IAM Idenity Center Instance ARN is configured.") logger.debug("Found IAM Idenity Center instance arn '%s'", instance_arn) permission_sets = list_permission_sets(instance_arn) logger.debug("Permission sets found '%s'", permission_sets) account_id, permission_set_arn, account_name = None, None, None for account in accounts: logger.info("Id of desired account is '%s' ", account["id"]) account_id = account.get("id") account_name = account.get("name") if account_id is None: logger.error("Can't find desired account '%s'", account_name) raise Exception("Clould not find account") for name, arn in permission_sets.items(): if name == permission_set_name: logger.info("ARN of desired permission set is '%s'", arn) permission_set_arn = arn if permission_set_arn is None: logger.error("Can't find desired permission set %s", permission_set_arn) raise Exception("Can't find desired permission set") principal_id = event["detail"]["responseElements"]["group"]["groupId"] logger.info("PrincipalId of the group is: %s", principal_id) if principal_id is None: logger.error("Could not retrieve the princiapal id of group %s", principal_id) raise Exception("Could not retrieve the princial id of the group") request = { "InstanceArn": instance_arn, "TargetId": account_id, "TargetType": "AWS_ACCOUNT", "PermissionSetArn": permission_set_arn, "PrincipalType": "GROUP", "PrincipalId": principal_id, #AWS IAM Identity Center group identifier } cracct_response = sso_admin_client.create_account_assignment(**request) cracct_request_id = cracct_response["AccountAssignmentCreationStatus"][ "RequestId" ] for tries in range(5): # The docs explain the following valid status states "IN_PROGRESS"|"FAILED"|"SUCCEEDED" ps_prov_set_status = ( sso_admin_client.describe_account_assignment_creation_status( InstanceArn=instance_arn, AccountAssignmentCreationRequestId=cracct_request_id, ) ) logger.info("Assignment attempt %s", tries) status = ps_prov_set_status["AccountAssignmentCreationStatus"]["Status"] if status == "IN_PROGRESS": logger.info("Assignment is in progress") logger.info("Sleeping for 5 seconds") sleep(5.0) continue if status == "FAILED": logger.error("Account assigned has failed") raise Exception("Account assignment has failed") return logger.info( "SUCCESS: Security Group: %s assigned to Account: %s with permission set: %s", group_display_name, account_name, permission_set_name, ) except Exception as err: message = {"Exception Details": str(err), "event": event} return message sns_topic = os.getenv("SNS_TOPIC") topic = sns_resource.Topic(sns_topic) topic.publish( Message=json.dumps(message), Subject="Account Association Operation Failed: SSO Automation", ) logger.info("Error notification sent via SNS") Handler: 'assign_group_to_account.lambda_handler' Role: !GetAtt ExecutionRole.Arn Runtime: 'python3.9' MemorySize: 128 Timeout: 900 Environment: Variables: SNS_TOPIC: !Ref AssignmentTopic INSTANCE_ARN: !Ref InstanceArn EventsFunctionPermission: Type: AWS::Lambda::Permission Properties: Action: lambda:InvokeFunction FunctionName: !GetAtt SsoAssignGroupsFunction.Arn Principal: events.amazonaws.com SourceArn: !GetAtt NewSSOGroupEventRule.Arn

参考

有关如何设置外部身份提供商配置以进行预置的详细信息，请参阅AWS Identity Center 自动预置文档。



原文:

The post introduced a solution for automating AWS Identity Center groups to AWS accounts and permission set assignments for groups created within AWS Identity Center from an external Identity Provider.

This post assumes a familiarity with the AWS IAM service and concepts such as roles and policies.

Introduction to Central Identity and Access Control on AWS

AWS multi-account environments are commonly setup with central identity and access control instead of doing this on a per individual account basis. AWS offers the IAM Identity Center (formerly Single Sign On) service for centralised access control, permissions and access to AWS accounts.

Identity Center supports bringing an organisation's existing users and groups into the AWS environment. IAM's documentation refers to this as using an external identity provider (IdP) to federate users and groups with Identity Center.

Within Identity Center permissions are managed via permission sets, which in turn are a collection of IAM policies. Once a permission set has been assigned for a user or group to an account, Identity Center will automatically create IAM roles in the account. The role's policies are configured from the permission set. In addition the role has a trust policy configured that allows the role to be only assumed when the user has been authenticated from the federated identity provider.

Managing AWS access control and permissions should always follow the least privilege permissions concept for any task for the environment's users. AWS environments typically are setup for role based access control where roles have sufficient permissions setup to accomplish the required tasks. Roles in the AWS Identity Center are commonly mapped to federated groups from the IdP. Depending on the requirements for granularity this potentially results in a 1:1 relationship between an IdP's groups and the combination of AWS account and permission set.

Let's look at a practical example. The AWS account 'app1-prod' should be accessed by the operators of the account using three different permissions depending on the tasks at hand.

For day to day regular operations most tasks should be read only and fall within the category of visibility, monitoring and observation. One can use AWS' provided AWS managed policy ReadOnlyAccess and the permissions defined by the policies would get the job done.

For emergency changes to the environment and management of the IAM service within the account the policies from AWS's managed AdministratorAccess policy can be used.

For any other tasks requiring interactive changes to the environment permissions defined the policies from AWS's managed PowerUserAccess policy can be used.

In the example scenario the IdP would have to hold three groups, giving the group's members access to the AWS account app1-prod with the three permission sets ReadOnlyAccess, AdministratorAccess and PowerUserAccess.

Creating the groups in the IdP and assignment of the permission sets to the AWS accounts for the group must be accomplished first before users from the groups can log into the AWS account.

Many organizations have automated AWS account creation since new applications and workloads need to be accommodated frequently. It is not uncommon for a workload to be deployed across several AWS accounts, each requiring IAM Identity Center setup before being handed over to the users of the accounts.

AWS does not provide an out of the box solution for automating the creation groups and permission sets to AWS account assignments and thus frequently this step is performed manually introducing delays and additional work.

Solution

This post describes a solution using AWS serverless resources for AWS Identity Center federated group to account and permission set assignment automation. This solution assumes that the naming of the group follows a regular pattern that contains the target AWS account and permission set as part of the name of the group.

As an example we will use the group name aws_accountname_a. The solution implementation is built with the following assumptions that the group name encodes:

  • A constant prefix for groups for AWS account access (the string aws_)
  • The account name where the group should be assigned to (the string accountname - any non white space characters between the _ characters of the whole group name)
  • A short name of the permission set (the string _p to refer to for AWSPowerUserAccess permission set) that the group should be assigned with on the account

Prerequisites

  • The user has an AWS account that has IAM Identity Center enabled.
  • The user has IAM Identity Center configured to use an external identity provider for user and group federation.
  • The external identity provider is configured, and set up for automatic provisioning, see the references section of this post.
  • The user has adequate permissions to manage resources within the account (using CloudFormation or using other methods).

High Level Architecture

The following diagram shows a high level architecture of the solution.

Workflow of the Solution

The basic work flow of the solution is highlighted in the following steps:

  • The external identity provider provisions a group within the AWS IAM Identity Center using the SCIM protocol between the identity provider and AWS IAM Identity Center, triggering an AWS CloudTrail event.
  • A AWS EventBridge rule monitors for the event raised upon provisioning and triggers an AWS Lambda function, passing the event details to the function.
  • The function decodes the group name from the event detail as it contains the desired accout name, the desired permission set name to be assigned to the group and performs the assignment within IAM Identity Center API.
  • The process is logged in an AWS CloudWatch log group for auditing and debugging purposes.
  • In case of failure an email will be dispatched.

Implementation

The solution is implemented in AWS CloudFormation but should be fairly portable to be implemented in other Infrastructure as Code frameworks such as Terraform, etc.

The main components are the EventBridge rule to listen for the desired event, the Lambda function and it's IAM permissions and the SNS component for sending email on failure.

CloudFormation Template

The solution ships as a single CloudFormation template that should be fairly easy to deploy to the AWS account that has Identity Center enabled.

Description: AWS SSO Automation Components

Parameters:
  ManagedResourcePrefix:
    Type: String
  InstanceArn:
    Type: String
  SMTPNotifyAddress:
    Type: String
  TopicName:
    Type: String
    Default: "AssigmentTopic"

Resources:
  NewSSOGroupEventRule:
    Type: AWS::Events::Rule
    Properties:
      Description: Trigger for when a new SSO Group is propagated from external IdP via SCIM
      EventPattern:
        source:
        - aws.sso-directory
        detail-type:
        - AWS API Call via CloudTrail
        detail:
          eventSource:
          - sso-directory.amazonaws.com
          eventName:
          - CreateGroup
      Targets:
        - Arn: !GetAtt SsoAssignGroupsFunction.Arn
          Id: sso-assign-group-function
  ExecutionRole:
    Type: AWS::IAM::Role
    Properties:
      AssumeRolePolicyDocument:
        Version: 2012-10-17
        Statement:
        - Effect: Allow
          Principal:
            Service: lambda.amazonaws.com
          Action:
          - sts:AssumeRole
      ManagedPolicyArns:
      - arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole
      Policies:
      - PolicyName: SSOandOrgPermissions
        PolicyDocument:
          Version: 2012-10-17
          Statement:
          - Effect: Allow
            Action:
            - sso-directory:Describe*
            - sso-directory:Get*
            - sso-directory:List*
            - sso-directory:Describe*
            - sso-directory:Search*
            - sso:Describe*
            - sso:Get*
            - sso:List*
            - sso:CreateAccountAssignment
            - sso:ProvisionPermissionSet
            - identitystore:List*
            - identitystore:Describe*
            - organizations:ListAccounts
            - sns:Publish
            Resource: '*'
  AssignmentTopic:
    Type: AWS::SNS::Topic
    Properties:
      Subscription:
        - Endpoint: !Ref SMTPNotifyAddress
          Protocol: "email"
      TopicName: !Ref TopicName
  SsoAssignGroupsFunction:
    Type: AWS::Lambda::Function
    Properties:
      Code:
        ZipFile: |
          import re
          import os
          import logging
          import json
          from time import sleep
          import boto3
          import traceback

          logger = logging.getLogger()
          logger.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)


          # This function assumes that groups are named like aws_<account-name>_<a|r|p>
          # see the documentation of this function for details.
          CONST_PREFIX = "aws"
          g_account_pattern = rf"^{CONST_PREFIX}_(\S*)_(\S*)"

          PSET_NAME_MAPPING_DICT = {
              "r": "AWSReadOnlyAccess",
              "a": "AWSAdministratorAccess",
              "p": "AWSPowerUserAccess",
          }

          sso_admin_client = boto3.client("sso-admin")
          org_client = boto3.client("organizations")
          sns_resource = boto3.resource("sns")


          def list_permission_sets(sso_instance_arn) -> dict:
              """Returns a dictionary of permissionssets for a given SSO Instance."""
              perm_set_dict = {}
              response = sso_admin_client.list_permission_sets(InstanceArn=sso_instance_arn)
              results = response["PermissionSets"]
              while "NextToken" in response:
                  response = sso_admin_client.list_permission_sets(
                      InstanceArn=sso_instance_arn, NextToken=response["NextToken"]
                  )
                  results.extend(response["PermissionSets"])

              for permission_set in results:
                  perm_description = sso_admin_client.describe_permission_set(
                      InstanceArn=sso_instance_arn, PermissionSetArn=permission_set
                  )
                  perm_set_dict[perm_description["PermissionSet"]["Name"]] = permission_set
              return perm_set_dict


          def list_aws_accounts() -> list:
              """Returns a list of account dictionaries containing  name id of each account"""
              account_list = []
              paginator = org_client.get_paginator("list_accounts")
              page_iterator = paginator.paginate()

              for page in page_iterator:
                  for acct in page["Accounts"]:
                      # only add active accounts
                      if acct["Status"] == "ACTIVE":
                          data = {"name": acct["Name"], "id": acct["Id"]}
                          account_list.append(data)
              #logger.debug("List of accounts: %s", account_list)
              return account_list


          def lambda_handler(event, context):
              logger.debug("Invoked with event: %s", event)
              try:
                  group_display_name = event["detail"]["responseElements"]["group"]["displayName"]
                  if group_display_name == "":
                      logger.debug(
                          "Recieved SCIM CreateGroup event for roup name '%s'", group_display_name
                      )
                      raise Exception("Event did not contain the group display name property")

                  result = re.search(g_account_pattern, group_display_name)
                  print(result)
                  if not result:
                      logger.error(
                          "Security group: '%s' does not matching naming convention for account assignment. REGEX retourned matches: %s",
                          group_display_name, result,
                      )
                      raise Exception(
                          "Security group does not match convention for account assignment automation"
                      )

                  account_name = result.group(1)
                  short_name_pset = result.group(2)  # "a" "p" or "r"

                  if short_name_pset not in PSET_NAME_MAPPING_DICT:
                      logger.error(
                          "Short name '%s' for permission set is not known", short_name_pset
                      )
                      raise Exception("Short name for permission set is not known")

                  permission_set_name = PSET_NAME_MAPPING_DICT[short_name_pset]
                  logger.debug(
                      "Searching for account '%s' and permission set '%s'",
                      account_name,
                      permission_set_name,
                  )

                  accounts = list_aws_accounts()

                  instance_arn = os.getenv("INSTANCE_ARN")
                  logger.info("IAM Identity Center Instance ARN is configured '%s'", instance_arn)

                  if instance_arn is None:
                      raise Exception("No IAM Idenity Center Instance ARN is configured.")

                  logger.debug("Found IAM Idenity Center instance arn '%s'", instance_arn)
                  permission_sets = list_permission_sets(instance_arn)

                  logger.debug("Permission sets found '%s'", permission_sets)

                  account_id, permission_set_arn, account_name = None, None, None
                  for account in accounts:
                      logger.info("Id of desired account is '%s' ", account["id"])
                      account_id = account.get("id")
                      account_name = account.get("name")

                  if account_id is None:
                      logger.error("Can't find desired account '%s'", account_name)
                      raise Exception("Clould not find account")

                  for name, arn in permission_sets.items():
                      if name == permission_set_name:
                          logger.info("ARN of desired permission set is '%s'", arn)
                          permission_set_arn = arn

                  if permission_set_arn is None:
                      logger.error("Can't find desired permission set %s", permission_set_arn)
                      raise Exception("Can't find desired permission set")

                  principal_id = event["detail"]["responseElements"]["group"]["groupId"]
                  logger.info("PrincipalId of the group is: %s", principal_id)
                  if principal_id is None:
                      logger.error("Could not retrieve the princiapal id of group %s", principal_id)
                      raise Exception("Could not retrieve the princial id of the group")

                  request = {
                      "InstanceArn": instance_arn,
                      "TargetId": account_id,
                      "TargetType": "AWS_ACCOUNT",
                      "PermissionSetArn": permission_set_arn,
                      "PrincipalType": "GROUP",
                      "PrincipalId": principal_id,  #AWS IAM Identity Center group identifier
                  }

                  cracct_response = sso_admin_client.create_account_assignment(**request)
                  cracct_request_id = cracct_response["AccountAssignmentCreationStatus"][
                      "RequestId"
                  ]

                  for tries in range(5):
                      # The docs explain the following valid status states "IN_PROGRESS"|"FAILED"|"SUCCEEDED"
                      ps_prov_set_status = (
                          sso_admin_client.describe_account_assignment_creation_status(
                              InstanceArn=instance_arn,
                              AccountAssignmentCreationRequestId=cracct_request_id,
                          )
                      )
                      logger.info("Assignment attempt %s", tries)
                      status = ps_prov_set_status["AccountAssignmentCreationStatus"]["Status"]

                      if status == "IN_PROGRESS":
                          logger.info("Assignment is in progress")
                          logger.info("Sleeping for 5 seconds")
                          sleep(5.0)
                          continue
                      if status == "FAILED":
                          logger.error("Account assigned has failed")
                          raise Exception("Account assignment has failed")
                          return

                  logger.info(
                      "SUCCESS: Security Group: %s assigned to Account: %s with permission set: %s",
                      group_display_name,
                      account_name,
                      permission_set_name,
                  )
              except Exception as err:
                  message = {"Exception Details": str(err),
                                    "event": event}
                  return message
                  sns_topic = os.getenv("SNS_TOPIC")
                  topic = sns_resource.Topic(sns_topic)
                  topic.publish(
                      Message=json.dumps(message),
                      Subject="Account Association Operation Failed: SSO Automation",
                  )
                  logger.info("Error notification sent via SNS")

      Handler: 'assign_group_to_account.lambda_handler'
      Role: !GetAtt ExecutionRole.Arn
      Runtime: 'python3.9'
      MemorySize: 128
      Timeout: 900
      Environment:
        Variables:
          SNS_TOPIC: !Ref AssignmentTopic
          INSTANCE_ARN: !Ref InstanceArn
  EventsFunctionPermission:
    Type: AWS::Lambda::Permission
    Properties:
      Action: lambda:InvokeFunction
      FunctionName: !GetAtt SsoAssignGroupsFunction.Arn
      Principal: events.amazonaws.com
      SourceArn: !GetAtt NewSSOGroupEventRule.Arn

References

See AWS Identity Center Automated Provisioning Docs for details on how to set a external Identity Provider configuration for provisioning.
item.author: grumpy_platform_engineer

item.title: 啤酒节回顾 | Hacktoberfest Recap
item.description:
亚历山大·萨马涅戈

发表于好吧，我的第一个 Hacktoberfest 已经结束了。 有时压力很大，但我认为这主要是因为这是我的第一个。 整个月的主要挑战是找到要解决的问题。 由于这是一项全球性活动，您必须快速和/或幸运地找到一个既具有挑战性又不会让像我这样的初学者不知所措的好问题。

总的来说，我在一个月内提出了四个拉取请求 (PR)。 可以在这里阅读：

我认为我的 PR 取得了很好的学习进展。 第一个 PR 只是让我参与其中并习惯于为公共开源项目做出贡献。 第二个 PR 是我通过修复项目中现有的错误来测试一些编码技能的地方。 它给了我一些研究潜在修复错误的经验，这些错误不是我自己创造的。 第三次 PR 让我能够继续我在同一个项目上的工作，并让我有机会增强该项目的一个功能。 这很有挑战性，因为我必须集思广益，为我寻找可能的解决方案提供我正在寻找的增强功能。 第四个也是最后一个 PR，虽然编码很简单，但让我体验到了探索不同类型的项目和技术堆栈的经验，如果我没有参加 Hacktoberfest，我可能不会探索这些。 找到好问题来解决问题具有挑战性，这一事实迫使我探索了许多项目来自己创建一个新问题。 这给了我很好的学习体验，因为我必须阅读和了解许多类型的技术堆栈才能更好地理解项目，以便我可以在存储库中创建问题。

总的来说，Hacktoberfest 让我有信心把自己放在那里，而不是害怕为开源项目做出贡献。 无论您的经验水平或问题的难易程度，每一次贡献都是一次学习经历。

一旦暂停，alexsam29 将无法评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被解除。

一旦解除暂停，alexsam29 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，alexsam29 的所有帖子都将被隐藏，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 alexsam29 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

感谢您保护 DEV 社区👩‍💻👨‍💻 的安全。 以下是标记 alexsam29 的方法：

举报其他不当行为

取消标记 alexsam29 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

Alexander Samaniego

Posted on Oct 30

Well my first ever Hacktoberfest is now over. It was stressful at times, but I think that was mostly just because it was my first one. The main challenge throughout the month was finding issues to work on. Since this is a global event, you had to be quick and/or lucky to find a good issue that was both challenging but yet, not overwhelming for a beginner like myself.

Overall, I made four pull requests (PRs) over the course of the month. Which can be read about here:

I think I made good learning progress with my PRs. The first PR was just me getting into the swing of things and getting used to contributing to a public open-source project. The second PR is where I actually got to put some coding skills to the test by fixing an existing bug in a project. It gave me some experience researching potential fixes to bugs that weren't my own creation. The third PR allowed me to continue with my work on the same project and gave me the opportunity to enhance a feature of the project. It was challenging as I had to brainstorm potential solutions that would give me the enhancement I was looking for. The fourth and final PR, while simple coding wise, gave me experience exploring different types of projects and tech stacks that I probably would not have explored had I not been participating in Hacktoberfest. The fact that it was challenging to find good issues to work on forced me to explore many projects to create a new issue myself. This gave me a good learning experience as I had to read and learn about many types of tech stacks in order to get a good understanding of the project, so that I could create an issue in the repository.

Overall, Hacktoberfest gave me the confidence to put myself out there and not to be afraid of contributing to open-source projects. No matter your experience level or how easy or difficult an issue is, every contribution is a learning experience.

If alexsam29 is not suspended, they can still re-publish their posts from their dashboard.

Report other inappropriate conduct

item.title: 为什么你没有减肥 | Why You Are Not Losing Weight
item.description:
马米诺游戏玩家

发表于减肥并不是所有健康问题的解决方案，但如果您的医生推荐，有一些提示可以帮助您安全减肥。 建议每周稳定减重 1 至 2 磅，以实现最有效的长期体重管理。

也就是说，许多旨在帮助您减轻体重的饮食计划会让您感到饥饿或不满意，或者它们会切断主要食物组并且不可持续。 这些是您可能很难坚持更健康的饮食计划的主要原因。

每个人都有独特的需求，不同的饮食方式和技巧可能比其他人更适合您。

您可能会发现您可以通过遵循低碳水化合物饮食或专注于全食的饮食来减轻体重，但是当您尝试减肥时，有一些适用的一般原则。

这里有科学依据

减少食欲和饥饿感，同时让您保持满意随着时间的推移持续减轻体重，同时有助于改善您的新陈代谢健康

如果你想快速减肥，这些技巧中的一些可能会有所帮助，但快速减肥很少是可持续的。 关注长期健康和可以坚持的习惯将有助于改善您的健康，更有可能导致持久的体重减轻。

如果 maminougamer 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

举报其他不当行为

原文:

maminou gamer

Posted on Oct 30

Weight loss is not the answer to every health problem, but if your doctor recommends it, there are tips to help you lose weight safely. A steady weight loss of 1 to 2 pounds per week is recommended for the most effective long-term weight management.

That said, many eating plans designed to help you lose weight leave you feeling hungry or unsatisfied, or they cut out major food groups and are not sustainable. These are major reasons why you might find it hard to stick to a healthier eating plan.

Everyone has unique needs and different eating styles and tips may work better for you than someone else.

You may find you are able to lose weight by following a low carb diet or a diet that has a focus on whole foods, but there are some general principles that apply when you’re trying to lose weight.

Here are science-backed

) tips to help you lose weight that include healthy eating, choosing carbs carefully, and that aim to:

reduce your appetite and hunger levels while keeping you satisfied produce consistent weight loss over time help improve your metabolic health at the same time

If you want to lose weight quickly, some of these tips may help, but quick weight loss is rarely sustainable. Focusing on long-term health and habits that you can stick with over time will help improve your health and are more likely to result in lasting weight loss.

Krzysztof Sordyl - Oct 30

item.title: 在 Angular 的帮助下向 If 语句添加结构 | Adding Structure to If Statements with help from Angular
item.description:
我发现自己在一个使用 Angular 构建的可观的单页应用程序的团队中工作。 在整个过程中，我们经常遇到具有多个决策点的场景，有时会导致一个文件中有很多 if 语句 - 鉴于随着代码库的增长，这是不可持续的，我们决定从条件逻辑迁移到多态性。

在构建具有大量传入数据的 UI 功能时，以及根据用户搜索的类别和细化而有很大差异的显示逻辑时，事情很容易变得混乱和复杂。 挑战之一是弄清楚如何保留一些结构，以允许某些业务区域具有不影响其他业务领域的自定义显示逻辑。

为了实现这一点，我们决定采用一种方法，在 Trade Me 的上下文中称为card-switcher 。 这允许我们在共享卡类型中拥有许多不同类型的显示逻辑。 如果出现了一种需要其自己的自定义显示逻辑的新类型的搜索，则可以通过card-switcher轻松添加和呈现，而不会影响任何现有的卡片组件。

作为一个额外的好处， card-switcher模式帮助我们保持我们的组件相当简单，这使得单元测试变得简单。 以及容易注意到和推理的错误。

在我们的应用程序中使用依赖注入

如果你在网上看，有一些很好的资源可以用来学习 Angular 应用程序中的依赖注入，还有很多有助于理解这个概念的图表。 官方的Angular 文档也是一个很好的起点——然而，当我们弄清楚我们的card-switcher中发生了什么时，我们还有更多的事情要做。

如果我们使用providedIn: root注册一个依赖项，那么它将在根注入器中注册。 这对于我们的大多数依赖项来说非常有用，因为这意味着如果应用程序不同部分的两个组件请求该依赖项，它们将获得相同的实例。

然而，在我们最近的搜索案例中，我们的组件依赖于抽象（ card-switcher ）。 该抽象在不同的​​垂直领域有不同的实现。 我们如何管理这一点是我们使用注入令牌来依赖该抽象。

我们不能在根注入器中注册我们的具体实现，因为它们需要使用相同的注入令牌进行注册。 如果您尝试在同一个令牌下注册两次（不指定 multi: true ）或最后一个获胜（这可能会引入不需要的副作用），则会出现错误 - 在我们的例子中，我们想要指定multi: true 。

通过这种方式，我们确保像card-switcher这样的组件可以存在于 main（中​​央文件位置）中，但依赖于垂直特定的实现。

元件厂



原文:

I've found myself in a team working on a considerable, single page application that is built using Angular. Throughout this we often come across scenarios which have multiple decision points sometimes resulting in a lot of if statements in one file - given this is not sustainable with a growing code base we decided to migrate from conditional logic to polymorphism.

When building a UI feature that has lots of incoming data, as well as display logic which varies greatly depending on the category and refinements of the users search, things can easily get messy and complex. One of the challenges was figuring out how to retain some structure which allows certain area's of the business to have custom display logic that doesn't affect the other areas of business.

To achieve this, we decided to go with an approach which in our context here at Trade Me is called a card-switcher. This allows us to have many different kinds of display logic within a shared card type. If a new kind of search comes out that requires its' own custom display logic, this can easily be added and rendered by the card-switcher without affecting any of the existing cards components.

As an added bonus, the card-switcher pattern helps us keep our components fairly simple which makes unit testing them straight forward. As well as bugs easy to notice and reason about.

The two main concepts from the Angular Framework that help us achieve this approach are dependancy injection and the component factory.

Using dependancy injection in our app

If you look online, there are some good resources for learning about dependancy injection in an angular application and lots of diagrams that are helpful to to understand the concept. The official Angular docs are a good place to start - as well However when working out what is going on in our card-switcher we have a bit more going on.

Angular has a hierarchical tree of injectors, with the root injector at its root. If we register a dependency with providedIn: root, then it will be registered at the root injector. This is great for most of our dependencies, because it means that if two components in different parts of the application ask for that dependency, they will get the same instance.

However in our recent searches case we have the case that our components have dependencies on an abstraction (card-switcher). That abstraction has different implementations in different verticals. How we manage this is we use an injection token to be able to depend on that abstraction.

We can't register our concrete implementations in the root injector because they need to be registered with the same injection token. There will either be an error if you try to register things twice under the same token (without specifying multi: true) or the last one wins (which could introduce unwanted side effects) - in our case we want to specify multi: true.

This way we make sure that components like the card-switcher can live in main (a central file location) but depend on a vertical specific implementation.

Component factory
item.author: skyebelinda

item.title: Python 的秘密武器：生成器 | Python's secret weapon: generators
item.description:
亚历克斯踏步

发表于根据定义，生成器是一个返回可迭代对象的函数。

换句话说，它是一个可用于创建列表的函数，但不是一次返回所有值，而是一次生成一个。

这听起来可能不是很令人兴奋，但生成器在许多情况下都非常有用。

例如，假设您有大量需要以某种方式处理的对象。

如果您要一次处理整个列表，它会占用大量内存并可能会减慢您的程序。

但是，如果您要使用生成器，则可以一次处理一个对象，这样会更有效率。
此外，生成器可用于创建无限序列。

这在您需要生成大量数据但又不想将其全部存储在内存中的情况下非常有用。

例如，您可以使用生成器来生成随机数流。

生成器是一个非常通用的工具，每个 Python 程序员都应该熟悉。

因此，下次当您发现自己需要处理大量数据或生成无限序列时，请不要忘记使用生成器！

米尔扎 Sisictester - 一旦暂停，treadalex 将无法评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被解除。

一旦解除暂停，treadalex 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦未发布，所有由treadalex 发布的帖子都将被隐藏，只有他们自己可以访问。

举报其他不当行为

取消标记的treadalex 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

Alex Tread

Posted on Oct 30

Python generators are powerful, but an often underutilized tool.

By definition, a generator is a function that returns an iterable object.

In other words, it's a function that can be used to create a list, but instead of returning all the values at once, it yields them one at a time.

This may not sound very exciting, but generators can be extremely helpful in a number of situations.

For example, let's say you have a large list of objects that you need to process in some way.

If you were to process the entire list at once, it would take up a lot of memory and could potentially slow down your program.

However, if you were to use a generator, you could process the objects one at a time, which would be much more efficient.
In addition, generators can be used to create infinite sequences.

This is extremely useful in situations where you need to generate a lot of data, but don't want to store it all in memory.

For instance, you could use a generator to generate a stream of random numbers.

Generators are a very versatile tool that every Python programmer should be familiar with.

So next time you find yourself needing to process a large amount of data or generate an infinite sequence, don't forget to reach for a generator!

Roshan Shambharkar - Oct 23

Mirza Sisictester - Oct 17

item.title: 从零开始 Terraform | Start Terraform from zero
item.description:
加里·肖

发表于准备：
提供者
环境的提供者永远是第一位的。 我们以 Azure Provider 为例。 我们可以创建一个“provider.tf”来覆盖配置：

 # We strongly recommend using the required_providers block to set the # Azure Provider source and version being used terraform { required_providers { azurerm = { source = "hashicorp/azurerm" version = ">=3.0.0" } } } # Configure the Microsoft Azure Provider provider "azurerm" { features {} } # Create a resource group resource "azurerm_resource_group" "example" { name = "example-resources" location = "West Europe" } # Create a virtual network within the resource group resource "azurerm_virtual_network" "example" { name = "example-network" resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.example.name location = azurerm_resource_group.example.location address_space = ["10.0.0.0/16"] }

提供者数据
如何访问当前的提供者数据？ 那是数据源：azurerm_client_config。 定义数据并像代码所示那样使用它。

 data "azurerm_client_config" "current" { } output "account_id" { value = data.azurerm_client_config.current.client_id }

资源定义您想要的资源，例如： 

 # Resource Group # https://registry.terraform.io/providers/hashicorp/azurerm/latest/docs/resources/resource_group resource "azurerm_resource_group" "example" { name = "example" location = "West Europe" } # Key Vault resource "azurerm_key_vault" "kv" { name = "KV" location = azurerm_resource_group.example.location resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.example.name enabled_for_disk_encryption = false enabled_for_deployment = true enabled_for_template_deployment = true tenant_id = data.azurerm_client_config.current.tenant_id sku_name = "standard" network_acls { default_action = "Allow" bypass = "AzureServices" } } # Service bus namespace resource "azurerm_servicebus_namespace" "example" { name = "example" resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.example.name location = azurerm_resource_group.example.location sku = "Standard" } # Service bus topic resource "azurerm_servicebus_topic" "example" { name = "example" namespace_id = azurerm_servicebus_namespace.metro60_namespace.id enable_partitioning = true }

变量
模块接受的每个输入变量都必须使用变量块声明：

 variable "image_id" { type = string description = "The id of the machine image (AMI) to use for the server." default = "abc" validation { condition = length(var.image_id) > 4 && substr(var.image_id, 0, 4) == "ami-" error_message = "The image_id value must be a valid AMI id, starting with \"ami-\"." } } # Local variables within modules locals { image_id_len = length(var.image_id) }

然后我们可以在代码中使用“var.image_id”和“local.image_id_len”作为机构。

举报其他不当行为

取消标记 garryxiao 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

Garry Xiao

Posted on Oct 30

Terraform - Automate Infrastructure on Any Cloud. https://www.terraform.io/

Preparation:
Visual Studio Code + HashiCorp Terraform extension

Provider
Provider for the environment is always in the first position. We take Azure Provider as an example. The Azure Provider can be used to configure infrastructure in Microsoft Azure using the Azure Resource Manager API's. https://registry.terraform.io/providers/hashicorp/azurerm/latest/docs. We could create a "provider.tf" to cover the configuration:

# We strongly recommend using the required_providers block to set the
# Azure Provider source and version being used
terraform {
  required_providers {
    azurerm = {
      source  = "hashicorp/azurerm"
      version = ">=3.0.0"
    }
  }
}

# Configure the Microsoft Azure Provider
provider "azurerm" {
  features {}
}

# Create a resource group
resource "azurerm_resource_group" "example" {
  name     = "example-resources"
  location = "West Europe"
}

# Create a virtual network within the resource group
resource "azurerm_virtual_network" "example" {
  name                = "example-network"
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.example.name
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.example.location
  address_space       = ["10.0.0.0/16"]
}

Provider Data
How to access current provider data? That's Data Source: azurerm_client_config. https://registry.terraform.io/providers/hashicorp/azurerm/latest/docs/data-sources/client_config. Define the data and use it like the code shows.

data "azurerm_client_config" "current" {
}

output "account_id" {
  value = data.azurerm_client_config.current.client_id
}

Resources Define resources you want, like:

# Resource Group
# https://registry.terraform.io/providers/hashicorp/azurerm/latest/docs/resources/resource_group
resource "azurerm_resource_group" "example" {
  name     = "example"
  location = "West Europe"
}

# Key Vault
resource "azurerm_key_vault" "kv" {
  name                            = "KV"
  location                        = azurerm_resource_group.example.location
  resource_group_name             = azurerm_resource_group.example.name
  enabled_for_disk_encryption     = false
  enabled_for_deployment          = true
  enabled_for_template_deployment = true
  tenant_id                       = data.azurerm_client_config.current.tenant_id

  sku_name = "standard"
  network_acls {
    default_action = "Allow"
    bypass         = "AzureServices"
  }
}

# Service bus namespace
resource "azurerm_servicebus_namespace" "example" {
  name                = "example"
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.example.name
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.example.location
  sku                 = "Standard"
}

# Service bus topic
resource "azurerm_servicebus_topic" "example" {
  name                = "example"
  namespace_id        = azurerm_servicebus_namespace.metro60_namespace.id
  enable_partitioning = true
}

Variables
https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/language/values/variables

Each input variable accepted by a module must be declared using a variable block:

variable "image_id" {
  type        = string
  description = "The id of the machine image (AMI) to use for the server."
  default     = "abc"
  validation {
    condition     = length(var.image_id) > 4 && substr(var.image_id, 0, 4) == "ami-"
    error_message = "The image_id value must be a valid AMI id, starting with \"ami-\"."
  }
}

# Local variables within modules
locals {
  image_id_len = length(var.image_id)
}

Then we could use "var.image_id" and "local.image_id_len" for institution in the codes.

Git ignore

# Exclude all .tfvars files, which are likely to contain sensitive data, such as
# password, private keys, and other secrets. These should not be part of version 
# control as they are data points which are potentially sensitive and subject 
# to change depending on the environment.
*.tfvars
*.tfvars.json

# Local .terraform directories
**/.terraform/*

# Local .tfstate files
*.tfstate
*.tfstate.*
**/override.tf
*.terraform.lock.hcl

item.title: Ruby on Rails 7 与 Ajax (axios) | Ruby on Rails 7 with Ajax (axios)
item.description:
格热戈日·斯迈多尔

发表于最近我在使用 Ruby on Rails 7，需要从 javascript 到我的 ROR 后端执行 API 请求。 过去我为此选择[axios](https://github.com/axios/axios) ，但我还是决定使用它（因为我还不知道 Turbo）。

该功能是从模式检查用户是否存在于数据库中。

在./bin/importmap pin axios并构建刺激控制器之后，我在浏览器中遇到错误：

TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading
'FormData at index.js:162:32

我开始用谷歌搜索，并在 stackoverflow 上搜索解决方案，但找不到任何有用的东西。

几分钟后......没有任何成功，我只是决定尝试不同版本的axios并猜猜是什么......原来0.27.2版本按预期工作

固定版本 0.27.2后
./bin/importmap pin axios@0.27.2

我在这里分享这个，因为你可能会发现它很有用！

您可以在介质上找到更长的阅读时间

一切顺利！
格热戈日

举报其他不当行为

原文:

Grzegorz Smajdor

Posted on Oct 30

Recently I was working with Ruby on Rails 7 and needed to do an API requests from javascript to my ROR backend. In the past I choose [axios](https://github.com/axios/axios) for that and still, I decided to use it (as I don't yet know Turbo).

The feature was to check if user exists in the DB from the modal.

After ./bin/importmap pin axios and building the stimulus controller I got an error in a browser:

TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading
'FormData at index.js:162:32

I started to google, and search on stackoverflow for the solution but could not find anything useful.

After few minutes ... without any success I just decided to experiment with different versions of axios and guess what...turns out that the version 0.27.2 was working as expected

After pinning the version 0.27.2
./bin/importmap pin axios@0.27.2

The AJAX request worked like charm! I'm sharing this here, as You might find it useful!

The longer read you can find on medium

All the best!
Grzegorz

item.title: Hacktoberfest 第 4 周：发现和创造问题。 | Hacktoberfest Week 4: Finding and Creating Issues.
item.description:
亚历山大·萨马涅戈

发表于在 Hacktoberfest 的第四周，我在存储库中搜索并创建了自己的问题，而不是查找现有问题。

项目回顾

我选择的项目是一个相当简单的项目。 这是一个旨在测试和练习打字技巧的网络应用程序。 用户可以参加和练习打字测试，然后将结果保存在本地。

问题和拉取请求

最初，与上周相比，我试图找到一个在编码方面更具挑战性的问题。 然而，在 Hacktoberfest 的最后阶段，我很难找到一个具有挑战性并且可以用我目前的技能组合实际可行的问题。 问题是我主要遇到的存储库不是真正的开源项目，而是专门为 Hactoberfest 拉取请求 (PR) 创建的。 当我确实找到了我想要的问题时，我没有足够快的速度发表评论，因为其他人已经被分配了任务。 因此，我花了太长时间在 GitHub 上搜索问题。

挑战

为了仍然给自己一个挑战，我决定创建自己的问题，而不是依赖已经制作的问题。 为此，我必须查找、分叉、设置、运行和探索许多（许多）项目，以找到我想添加的潜在错误和/或功能。 我必须学习许多技术堆栈并阅读无数文档页面才能让这些项目运行并了解它们是如何工作的。

这就是本周的 PR 比前几周更具挑战性的原因，因为在此之前，错误修复和功能请求已经由其他人提出。 我所要做的就是找到一种方法来修复/添加它。 现在，我必须寻找错误并思考新功能。 因为你觉得别人会因为某事评判你，所以对创造新问题有额外的“新手恐惧”； 比如可能在文档中遗漏某些内容，或者让您的功能请求被其他人视为“愚蠢”。 有时我认为我发现了一个错误，但这只是我的设置错误。 或者有时我会提出一个问题，但项目维护者会否认或提出其他建议。

一旦我通过了最具挑战性的部分并得到分配给我的一个我创建的问题，我就开始工作了。 我接到的任务是在 Typing Test 项目中添加一个新表格，显示所有打字测试的平均速度和时间。 老实说，这个问题并没有太大的挑战性。 我通过计算 JavaScript 文件中的平均时间并使用 DOM 在现有表格下方添加一个新表格来添加新功能。 我创建了 PR并将功能添加到项目中，只需要很少的调整。

结论

虽然实际的编码并不像我最初希望的那样具有挑战性，但我仍然很高兴我获得了这种经验，因为自己创建一个新问题的挑战让我有机会探索许多我以前可能不会看过的项目。 我获得了不同技术堆栈的知识，这让我有了新的信心，不害怕在开源项目中创建新问题。

丹尼斯拉夫·加夫里洛夫👨‍🚀🤖🤷‍♂️ - 一旦暂停，alexsam29 将无法评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被解除。

原文:

Alexander Samaniego

Posted on Oct 30

For the fourth week of Hacktoberfest, I searched for and created my own issue in a repository, rather than finding an existing issue.

Project Recap

The project I chose is a rather simple project. It's a web application designed to test and practice typing skills. User's can take and practice typing tests and the results will then be saved locally.

The Issue & Pull Request

Initially, I tried to find an issue that was more challenging coding wise when compared to last week. However, I struggled to find an issue that was challenging and realistically do-able with my current skill set this late into Hacktoberfest. The problem was that I mainly encountered repositories that weren't real open-source projects and were created solely for Hactoberfest pull requests (PRs). When I did find an issue that I wanted, I was not quick enough to comment as somebody else was already assigned the task. Due to this, I spent way too long just searching GitHub for issues.

The Challenge

In order to still give myself a challenge, I decided to create my own issue instead of relying on ones already made. To do this, I had to find, fork, setup, run, and explore many (many) projects to find potential bugs and or features I'd like to add. I had to learn many tech stacks and read countless of documentation pages to get these projects to run and know how they work.

This is what made this week's PR much more challenging than the previous weeks because before, the bug fixes and feature requests were already made by someone else. All I had to do was find a way to fix/add it. Now, I had to do the bug finding and thinking for new features. There was that additional 'rookie fear' of creating new issues because you get the feeling that others will judge you for something; like potentially missing something in the documentation or having your feature request be seen as 'dumb' by others. There were times where I thought I had found a bug, but it was just an error with setup on my part. Or times where I'd suggest an issue, but the project maintainers would deny or suggest something else.

Once I got past the most challenging part and got an issue that I created assigned to me, I got to work. The issue I got assigned was to add a new table in the Typing Test project that showed the average speed and time of all typing tests taken. I'll be honest, this issue wasn't too challenging. I added the new feature by calculating average times in the JavaScript files and adding a new table below the existing one using the DOM. I created the PR and got the feature added to the project with very little adjustments needed.

Conclusion

While the actual coding was not as challenging as I had initially hoped, I'm still glad I got this experience because the challenge of creating a new issue myself gave me a chance to explore many projects that I probably would not have looked at before. I gained knowledge on different tech stacks and it gave me new found confidence to not be afraid to create new issues in open-source projects.

Denislav Gavrilov 👨‍🚀🤖🤷‍♂️ - Oct 11

item.title: Hacktoberfest PR 3：改进文档 | Hacktoberfest PR 3: Improving documentation
item.description:
本周我有机会改进Telescope项目的开发者贡献文档。

概述

当我在我的机器上本地解决这个问题时，我遇到了一个问题，我无法看到任何博客文章，即使我使用environment-setup文件作为我设置 Telescope 的指南。 由于我试图修复的错误不需要访问博客提要，因此我在 GitHub 上提交了一个单独的问题。

问题

问题是environment-setup文件缺少有关正确播种 Telescope 使用的数据库的信息。 所需的信息在一个单独的文件中。

目标

我的目标是通过在一个文件environment-setup中正确集成与运行 Telescope 相关的所有信息来增强开发人员的体验。

信息已添加

结果

用户可以轻松导航到不同的文件来初始化数据库。 所有步骤都在一个文件中

感谢Josue ，感谢您的帮助和支持。
